title_en: Al-Qushayri and the Systematization of Sunni Spiritual Science
content_en:
Ar-Risalah al-Qushayriyyah — The Qushayri Epistle — is one of the most important texts in the history of Islamic spiritual science and the most influential attempt to systematize and defend the legitimacy of Sufi practice within the framework of orthodox Sunni theology. Its author, Abu al-Qasim Abd al-Karim al-Qushayri (986–1072 CE), was a scholar of exceptional breadth who mastered Shafi'i fiqh, Ash'ari theology, and the spiritual sciences, combining them in a comprehensive synthesis that set the standard for Sunni Sufism for centuries.

Al-Qushayri wrote his epistle in response to a crisis he perceived in the Sufi tradition of his era. In a famous preamble, he lamented that many who claimed the title of Sufi in his time had abandoned scholarly discipline, engaged in practices with no basis in Sunnah, and used spiritual claims as covers for moral laxity. His project was to rescue genuine Sufi spirituality — the cultivation of the heart in accordance with Quran and Sunnah — from the innovations and corruptions that had attached themselves to it, and to present an account of Sunni spiritual practice that could be recognized as sound by mainstream scholars of fiqh and theology.

The epistle is organized in several major sections. The first section provides biographical sketches of the great early Sufi masters — figures like al-Hasan al-Basri, Ibrahim ibn Adham, Bishr al-Hafi, Junayd al-Baghdadi, and many others — presenting them as models of combined scholarly and spiritual excellence. This section is important because it grounds the Sufi tradition in figures whose learning and piety were beyond question, establishing that the tradition at its best produced exemplary Muslims, not religious eccentrics.

The second major section is a systematic dictionary of spiritual terms — the technical vocabulary that had developed in the Sufi tradition to describe states, stations, and inner experiences of the spiritual path. Al-Qushayri's contribution is to define each term precisely, connect it to Quranic and hadith evidence, and situate it within the framework of Ash'ari theology. This systematic treatment of spiritual terminology was enormously influential and was cited by virtually every subsequent Sunni scholar who addressed the spiritual sciences.
